content:
# Genesis Chapter 33 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19r–19v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_033_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_033_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 30–31 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0034

---

**1** Then Jacob lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, Esau was coming, and four hundred men were with him. So he divided the children among Leah, Rachel, and the two female servants.

**2** He placed the female servants with their children in front, Leah and her children after them, and Rachel and Joseph last of all.

**3** He himself went on before them, and bowed to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother.

**4** But Esau ran to meet him and embraced him, and fell on his neck and kissed him, and they wept.

**5** Then Esau lifted up his eyes and saw the women and the children, and said, "Who are these with you?" Jacob said, "The children whom God has graciously given your servant."

**6** Then the female servants drew near, they and their children, and they bowed down.

**7** Leah also and her children drew near and bowed down. And afterward Joseph and Rachel drew near, and they bowed down.

**8** Esau said, "What do you mean by all this company that I met?" Jacob answered, "To find favor in the eyes of my lord."

**9** But Esau said, "I have enough, my brother; keep what you have for yourself."

**10** Jacob said, "No, please, if I have found favor in your eyes, then accept my present from my hand. For I have seen your face as one sees the face of God, and you have received me with favor.

**11** Please accept my gift that was brought to you, because God has dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough." Thus he urged him, and he took it.

**12** Then Esau said, "Let us journey on our way, and I will go before you."

**13** But Jacob said to him, "My lord knows that the children are frail, and the nursing flocks and herds are a care to me. If they are driven hard for one day, all the flocks will die.

**14** Let my lord pass on before his servant, and I will lead on slowly, at the pace of the livestock that are before me and at the pace of the children, until I come to my lord at Seir."

**15** Esau said, "Let me leave with you some of the people who are with me." But Jacob said, "What need is there? Let me find favor in the eyes of my lord."

**16** So Esau returned that day on his way to Seir.

**17** But Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built himself a house and made booths for his livestock. Therefore the name of the place is called Succoth.

**18** And Jacob came safely to the city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, when he came from Paddan-aram, and he camped before the city.

**19** And from the sons of Hamor, Shechem's father, he bought for a hundred pieces of money the piece of land on which he had pitched his tent.

**20** There he erected an altar and called it El-Elohe-Israel.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 33 spans Folios 19r–19v.

   **Folio 19r** (GEN_033_page_001.jpg): The recto of Folio 19. This page carries the final verse of Genesis 32 (the dietary prohibition concerning the sinew of the thigh) and then the full opening of Genesis 33. The chapter division is visible. Genesis 33 begins with Jacob's sighting of Esau approaching with four hundred men (v. 1), the arrangement of the household in order (vv. 1–2), Jacob's sevenfold prostration (v. 3), the celebrated reconciliation — Esau's run, embrace, kiss, and tears (v. 4), the introduction of the family (vv. 5–7), the exchange over the gift (vv. 8–11), and Esau's offer to journey together (vv. 12–15). The three-column layout with Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks is clearly legible. Masorah parva notes are visible in the inter-column margins. The extraordinary dots (*puncta extraordinaria*) over the word וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ (vayyishshaqehu — "and he kissed him") in verse 4 are visible in the manuscript as a row of dots above the letters of the word.

   **Folio 19v** (GEN_033_page_002.jpg): The verso of Folio 19. This page carries Genesis 33:16–20 — Esau's return to Seir (v. 16), Jacob's journey to Succoth (v. 17), Jacob's arrival at Shechem (v. 18), the purchase of land from the sons of Hamor (v. 19), and the erection of the altar called El-Elohe-Israel (v. 20) — and then continues into the opening verses of Genesis 34. The text of the chapter's closing section is clearly legible.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 30** (GEN_033_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 30 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0034). This page carries the end of the Genesis 32 material and the LXX text corresponding to the opening of Genesis 33. The continuous uncial Greek text in three columns is legible, carrying through approximately Genesis 33:1–11.

   **Page 31** (GEN_033_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 31 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0035). The chapter marker "CAP. XXXVIII" is visible at the right margin. This page carries the remainder of Genesis 33 (vv. 12–20) and continues into Genesis 34. The text is legible throughout.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "He lifted up his eyes and looked" — וַיִּשָּׂא יַעֲקֹב עֵינָיו וַיַּרְא (v. 1)**

The verb sequence וַיִּשָּׂא ... עֵינָיו (vayyissa einav — "and he lifted his eyes") followed by וַיַּרְא (vayyar — "and he saw/looked") is a characteristic Biblical Hebrew construction for deliberate, alert visual perception. The formal translation preserves "lifted up his eyes and looked" following the Hebrew precisely, in line with the governing principle established in the LOGOS translation: "if the source says 'he lifted his eyes,' do not write 'he looked up.'" The same construction appears in Genesis 18:2 (Abraham sees the three visitors), Genesis 22:4 (Abraham sees Moriah), and Genesis 43:29 (Joseph sees Benjamin) — it marks moments of heightened attention at turning points. The LXX renders ἀναβλέψας δὲ Ιακωβ τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς εἶδεν (anablepsas de Iakob tois ophthalmois eiden — "and Jacob, having looked up with his eyes, saw"), consistent with the MT.

**2. The Sevenfold Prostration — וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַרְצָה שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים (v. 3)**

Jacob bowed to the ground (אַרְצָה, artsah — literally "earthward," toward the ground) seven times (שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים, sheva pe'amim). The sevenfold prostration is attested in ancient Near Eastern diplomatic protocol — the Amarna letters from Canaan record vassals prostrating themselves "seven times and seven times" before the Pharaoh. Jacob's gesture is simultaneously diplomatic submission and personal penitence. The formal translation renders "bowed to the ground seven times" — the adverb "earthward" being captured in "to the ground." The LXX renders προσεκύνησεν ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν ἑπτάκις (prosekynesen epi ten gen heptakis — "he worshipped/bowed down upon the earth seven times"), consistent with the MT.

**3. The Puncta Extraordinaria on וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ (v. 4)**

Verse 4 records that Esau ran, embraced Jacob, fell on his neck, and kissed him — and they wept. The Masoretic tradition has placed dots (*puncta extraordinaria*) above the word וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ (vayyishshaqehu — "and he kissed him"), and these dots are visible on the Leningrad Codex image. Puncta extraordinaria are a scribal notation appearing fifteen times in the Torah and elsewhere, marking passages that the scribes considered textually unusual or theologically significant without altering the consonantal text. The dots do not indicate a deletion; they indicate scribal hesitation or wonder. Rabbinic interpretation split on the significance: some read them as indicating that Esau's kiss was insincere, kissing Jacob with biting rather than love (a pun on נָשַׁק, nashaq — "to kiss," and נָשַׁךְ, nashakh — "to bite"); others, including Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai, read them as indicating that in this moment Esau's kiss was genuine. The Sifrei notes that this is one of the five places in the Torah where the dots are meant to signal that the word's full force should be felt rather than reduced. The formal translation renders the kiss plainly — "kissed him" — without editorial interpretation, preserving the text as it stands and noting the scribal marking here rather than in the body of the translation. The LXX renders καὶ κατεφίλησεν αὐτόν (kai katephilesen auton — "and he kissed him tenderly/fervently"), with κατα- prefixed to the verb indicating a more intense or full kiss — the LXX resolves the ambiguity toward the sincere reading.

**4. "The face of God" — פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים (v. 10)**

Jacob's words to Esau in verse 10 are among the most theologically resonant in the chapter: "I have seen your face as one sees the face of God, and you have received me with favor." The phrase פְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים (penei Elohim — "the face of God") directly echoes Jacob's experience at Peniel in Genesis 32:30 — "I have seen God face to face" (רָאִיתִי אֱלֹהִים פָּנִים אֶל פָּנִים, ra'iti Elohim panim el panim). The comparison is not mere flattery; it is a theologically loaded statement. Jacob, who survived seeing the face of God at Peniel and expected death, now encounters the face of his brother and is received with favor instead of violence. The brother's welcoming face becomes, for Jacob, an icon of the divine face he has just survived. The formal translation preserves the full comparison — "as one sees the face of God" — following the Hebrew precisely. The LXX renders ὅτι εἶδον τὸ πρόσωπόν σου ὡς ἄν τις ἴδοι πρόσωπον θεοῦ (hoti eidon to prosōpon sou hōs an tis idoi prosōpon theou — "because I have seen your face as one might see the face of God"), consistent with the MT.

**5. "My gift" / "my blessing" — בִּרְכָתִי (v. 11)**

Jacob's request that Esau "accept my gift" uses the word בִּרְכָתִי (birkhati — "my blessing"). The noun בְּרָכָה (berakah) means both "blessing" and "gift" — the two concepts are overlapping in Hebrew. Most English translations render it "gift" or "present" here (ESV: "my gift"; NASB: "my gift"; NKJV: "my present"). However, the choice of birkhati rather than the standard word for gift (מִנְחָה, minchah, which has been used in the previous chapter) is almost certainly deliberate. Jacob stole Esau's blessing (birkat yitshaq — the blessing of Isaac — in Genesis 27). Here he returns to Esau something he calls by the same name: birkhati, "my blessing." The reconciliation involves, at a verbal level, the restoration of what was taken. The formal translation renders "my gift" following the contextual English convention for this usage of berakah, but this note flags the layered significance of the word choice. The LXX renders τὰς εὐλογίας μου (tas eulogias mou — "my blessings"), rendering berakah in its primary theological sense rather than its secondary meaning of "gift," and thus makes the Jacob-Esau blessing-theft connection explicit in the Greek.

**6. "God has dealt graciously with me" — חַנַּנִי אֱלֹהִים (v. 11)**

The verb חָנַן (chanan — "to be gracious, to show favor") in the perfect third person with God as subject — חַנַּנִי (channani — "God has been gracious to me" / "God has dealt graciously with me") — is the same root from which the name Hannah (חַנָּה) and the later concept of divine grace derive. Jacob's "I have enough" (יֶשׁ לִי כֹל, yesh li khol — "I have everything") echoes Esau's "I have enough" (יֶשׁ לִי רָב, yesh li rav — "I have much/plenty") in verse 9. The two brothers, each insisting they have sufficient, are standing on opposite sides of abundance — Esau's sufficiency is expressed in terms of quantity (rav — "much"), Jacob's in terms of totality (khol — "everything/all"). The formal translation preserves both: "I have enough" for Esau (v. 9) and "I have enough" for Jacob (v. 11), noting in this translation note that the two Hebrew terms are distinct. The LXX renders ἔστιν μοι πάντα (estin moi panta — "I have all things") for Jacob in verse 11, consistent with khol.

**7. "At the pace of" — לְרַגְלִי (v. 14)**

The phrase לְרַגְלִי (le-rageli — literally "at my foot" or "at the foot of") is used twice in verse 14 to describe the pace of travel: Jacob will "lead on slowly, at the pace of the livestock (לְרֶגֶל הַמְּלָאכָה, le-regel ha-melakhah — "at the foot of the work/livestock") and at the pace of the children (לְרֶגֶל הַיְלָדִים, le-regel ha-yeladim)." The idiom לְרֶגֶל (le-regel) in this context means "following at the foot of / at the pace of" — traveling at the speed dictated by those who are slower. The formal translation renders "at the pace of the livestock" and "at the pace of the children," which captures the meaning while maintaining the parallelism of the Hebrew construction. The LXX renders κατὰ τὴν σχολὴν τῆς ἐργασίας (kata ten scholēn tes ergasias — "according to the leisure of the work") and κατὰ τὴν σχολὴν τῶν παιδαρίων (kata ten scholēn tōn paidariōn — "according to the leisure of the children"), rendering the leisured pace concept.

**8. "Succoth" — סֻכּוֹת (v. 17)**

The place name סֻכּוֹת (Sukkot) is the plural of סֻכָּה (sukkah — "booth, shelter, leafy hut"). The text provides its own etymology: Jacob "made booths (sukkot) for his livestock; therefore the name of the place is called Sukkot." The formal translation retains "Succoth" as a proper name (the standard English form following the Greek Σοκχωθ / Sokkōth) and preserves "booths" as the translation of the common noun in the same verse, allowing the etymological play to be visible to the English reader. The annual festival of Sukkot (Feast of Booths / Tabernacles) later commanded in Leviticus 23 and Deuteronomy 16 will be named for these temporary shelters, drawing a line from Jacob's pastoral encampment to the liturgical life of Israel. The LXX renders Σοκχωθ (Sokkōth) as the place name and σκηνάς (skēnas — "tents/tabernacles") as the common noun, consistent with the MT's play on the word.

**9. "Jacob came safely to Shechem" — וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב שָׁלֵם (v. 18)**

This verse presents one of the most debated translation problems in Genesis 33. The text reads: וַיָּבֹא יַעֲקֹב שָׁלֵם עִיר שְׁכֶם (vayyavo Ya'aqov shalem ir Shekhem). The word שָׁלֵם (shalem) is ambiguous:

- As an **adverb/adjective**: "safely" or "in peace" or "whole/complete" — "Jacob came safely/in peace to the city of Shechem"
- As a **proper noun**: "Salem" — the name of a town — "Jacob came to Salem, a city of Shechem"

The LXX (Vaticanus) reads εἰς Σαλημ πόλιν Σικιμων (eis Salēm polin Sikimōn — "into Salem, the city of the Shechemites"), treating שָׁלֵם as the place name Salem. Jerome's Vulgate follows this reading with in Salim urbem Sichemitarum. However, the majority of modern formal translations (ESV, NASB, NIV, NKJV) render shalem as the adverb "safely" or "peacefully," producing "Jacob came safely to the city of Shechem." The Hebrew construction — שָׁלֵם without the definite article הַ (ha-) — could support either reading; place names do not require the article. The formal translation follows the adverbial reading ("safely") for several reasons: (1) the word shalem appears just before with its full adjectival/nominal force in v. 11 (yesh li khol — "I have all/enough"), and the theme of wholeness and completion runs through the chapter; (2) the narrative logic of Jacob's journey concluding "in peace/wholeness" provides theological closure to the Jabbok crisis; (3) there is no independent attestation of a town called Salem in this specific location distinct from Shechem itself. The translation note preserves the alternative reading. The LXX's "Salem" reading is noted here.

**10. The qesitah — קְשִׂיטָה (v. 19)**

The price paid for the land — מֵאָה קְשִׂיטָה (me'ah qesitah — "one hundred qesitah") — uses a term of monetary value that appears only three times in the entire Hebrew Bible: here, in Joshua 24:32 (the same field at Shechem), and in Job 42:11. The word קְשִׂיטָה (qesitah) is of uncertain derivation and its exact monetary value is unknown. It may relate to a weight of precious metal or an archaic coin equivalent. The formal translation renders "a hundred pieces of money" — following the traditional English equivalent (compare KJV "an hundred pieces of money") — as no specific English monetary term exists that would be more accurate. The LXX renders ἑκατὸν ἀμνῶν (hekaton amnōn — "one hundred lambs"), reading qesitah as a unit equivalent to a lamb — a distinct and ancient interpretive tradition that may preserve a memory of the term's original meaning (livestock as currency). The formal translation follows the MT's monetary sense rather than the LXX's ovine reading, noting the LXX here.

**11. "El-Elohe-Israel" — אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (v. 20)**

The altar name אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל (El Elohe Yisra'el) means "God, the God of Israel." This is the first time Jacob uses his new name — Israel — in a divine title, formally adopting the name given at Peniel (32:28) as the basis for a covenantal identification: the God he has wrestled with is specifically "the God of Israel." The name echoes the confession structure of patriarchal religion: "the God of Abraham," "the God of Isaac" — now "the God of Israel." The altar dedication is both a religious act and an identity declaration. The formal translation retains the untranslated form "El-Elohe-Israel" as a proper name, as this is the altar's name and its meaning is immediately transparent from the word-by-word rendering given above. The LXX renders Θεὸς ὁ θεὸς Ισραηλ (Theos ho theos Israēl — "God, the God of Israel"), a direct translation rather than a transliteration.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 33

Pages 30–31 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible. Key variants observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 33:4 | וַיִּשָּׁקֵהוּ with *puncta extraordinaria* above | καὶ κατεφίλησεν αὐτόν ("he kissed him fervently") | "and kissed him" | LXX resolves scribal ambiguity toward genuine kiss; formal translation renders plainly and notes the dots |
| 33:11 | בִּרְכָתִי ("my blessing/gift") | τὰς εὐλογίας μου ("my blessings") | "my gift" | LXX renders primary theological meaning "blessing"; MT contextually functions as "gift"; formal translation follows contextual English rendering with note on the theological layer |
| 33:17 | סֻכּוֹת / סֻכֹּת ("Succoth" / "booths") | Σοκχωθ / σκηνάς ("Sokkoth" / "tents/tabernacles") | "Succoth" / "booths" | MT and LXX consistent; formal translation preserves the etymological play |
| 33:18 | שָׁלֵם עִיר שְׁכֶם ("shalem/Salem, city of Shechem") | εἰς Σαλημ πόλιν Σικιμων ("into Salem, city of the Shechemites") | "safely to the city of Shechem" | LXX reads שָׁלֵם as proper noun Salem; MT grammatically allows either; formal translation follows adverbial/adjectival reading with note on the alternative |
| 33:19 | מֵאָה קְשִׂיטָה ("a hundred qesitah") | ἑκατὸν ἀμνῶν ("a hundred lambs") | "a hundred pieces of money" | Significant divergence: LXX interprets qesitah as a lamb; formal translation follows MT monetary sense; both readings preserve an ancient tradition |
| 33:20 | אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל ("El-Elohe-Israel") | Θεὸς ὁ θεὸς Ισραηλ ("God, the God of Israel") | "El-Elohe-Israel" (untranslated as altar name) | LXX translates the altar name; formal translation retains transliterated form as a proper name |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Reconciliation (vv. 1–16)**

Genesis 33 is structured as a mirror-image reversal of Genesis 27–28. In that earlier sequence: Jacob deceived his father, stole his brother's blessing, and fled in fear eastward. Here: Jacob returns from the east, sends gifts ahead, prostrates himself seven times in submission, and his brother runs to embrace him. The narrative deliberately inverts every element of the betrayal. Jacob, who had dressed in Esau's garments to deceive his father, now approaches his brother with visible humility. The brother who received the cursed position — "you will serve your brother" — now runs to embrace the servant who bows before him. The reconciliation is not narrated as dramatic or emotionally complex; it is rendered with remarkable economy. Esau runs, embraces, weeps. The restoration happens in three Hebrew words: וַיָּרָץ עֵשָׂו לִקְרָאתוֹ (vayyarats Esav liqrato — "and Esau ran to meet him"). After twenty years of narrative tension, the reunion resolves in a single verse.

**Jacob's Seven Prostrations and Esau's Seven Gifts (vv. 3, 8–11)**

The number seven structures the reconciliation scene. Jacob bows seven times (v. 3) — the number of completeness and covenant. The elaborate gift-caravan sent ahead in Genesis 32 was organized in waves: five droves of animals separated by spaces (32:16–21). The gift itself was calibrated as an act of appeasement (kapper — to cover/atone). In chapter 33, Esau's initial response "I have enough" and Jacob's counter-insistence that Esau accept the gift ("accept my blessing from my hand") completes the act. The exchange of words over the gift in verses 8–11 occupies more narrative space than the actual embrace, suggesting that the verbal reconciliation — Jacob naming his gift as birkhati ("my blessing") — carries particular weight.

**The Three Journeys of Verse 17–20: Separation, Settlement, Consecration**

The chapter's closing three verses are deceptively simple but theologically loaded:
- Jacob separates from Esau and goes to Succoth (v. 17) — temporary settlement, a camp of booths, west of the Jordan. He builds a house. He makes shelters for his animals. He is still not yet in the land proper.
- Jacob arrives "safely" at Shechem in Canaan (v. 18) — this is the land promised to Abraham. The word שָׁלֵם (shalem — "safely/whole/complete") connects to the שָׁלוֹם (shalom — "peace") that Jacob prayed for when he left Canaan (28:21 — "if I return in peace/shalom to my father's house"). The prayer of Bethel is answered.
- Jacob buys land and erects an altar (vv. 19–20) — this echoes Abraham's purchase of the cave of Machpelah (Genesis 23) and Abraham's altar-building at Shechem (Genesis 12:6–7). The patriarchal pattern of arrival → land purchase → altar erection is repeated. Jacob's altar name — El-Elohe-Israel — announces that the God of the patriarchs is now the God of Israel, the new name established at Peniel.

**The Qesitah and the Field of Shechem**

The field bought here for one hundred qesitah will become theologically significant: in Joshua 24:32, Joseph's bones (carried out of Egypt, per Genesis 50:25 and Exodus 13:19) are buried in this same field at Shechem — the burial is explicitly noted as being in "the piece of land that Jacob bought from the sons of Hamor the father of Shechem for one hundred pieces of money." The purchase in Genesis 33:19 is thus a long-range narrative marker, the land passing from Jacob through centuries to become Joseph's burial place in the land of promise. John 4:5 locates Jesus's conversation with the Samaritan woman near "the piece of land that Jacob gave to his son Joseph" — the New Testament narrative places a pivotal encounter at this same site.

---

— LOGOS
